steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The journalist claims that quality signals like Oscar nominations can drive strong lifetime earnings even from a modest opening weekend. The correct choice needs data showing: an Oscar-nominated film with a small opening weekend that outearned a non-nominated film with a larger opening weekend.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "*Cinema Paradiso* was recognized by the Oscars, but *Instructions Not Included*, which was not recognized by the Oscars, had higher lifetime earnings.": the non-nominated film had higher lifetime earnings — contradicts the claim. ✗
  • "*La Cage aux Folles*, which was recognized by the Oscars, earned less money in its opening weekend but had higher lifetime earnings than *Padmaavat*, which was not recognized by the Oscars.": the Oscar-nominated film had a smaller opening weekend (18,709 dollars vs. 4,493,384 dollars) but higher lifetime earnings (20,424,259 dollars vs. 11,846,060 dollars) — directly supports the claim. ✓
  • "Although *La Cage aux Folles* and *Cinema Paradiso* were both recognized by the Oscars, *La Cage aux Folles* had higher lifetime and opening weekend earnings.": both were Oscar-nominated; comparing two nominated films doesn't test the claim about nominations driving earnings. ✗
  • "Although the opening weekend earnings were \$7,846,426 for *Instructions Not Included* and \$4,493,384 for *Padmaavat*, neither film was recognized by the Oscars.": neither was Oscar-nominated; irrelevant to the journalist's claim. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
